overview: Azorius Control aims to manipulate the match from an early disadvantage to eventual inevitability by controlling the board and accumulating card advantage. It relies on a handful of powerful win conditions backed by extensive removal and interaction. The deck excels at answering aggressive strategies and combo decks while leveraging token synergy and planeswalker value to close out games.

keep_criteria: Has enough lands to develop the game plan
Contains a win condition and control pieces
Includes card advantage tools like Stock Up or Three Steps Ahead
Keep any hand with 2+ lands and at least one removal or counter spell.
Prioritize hands with 3+ lands if they contain a turn-2 play (cantrip, early interaction, or board wipe).
With WU colors, aim for 2-3 lands that enter untapped or can fetch/fix to ensure consistent second lands.
mulligan_criteria: Lacks enough lands to progress the game plan
Relies solely on card draw to find mana
Mulligan pure land hands, hands with only high-cost spells (4+ mana), or hands lacking interaction if the metagame is aggressive.

examples.reason: Has enough lands to develop the game plan, a win condition, and control pieces. Three Steps Ahead provides card advantage.
Very solid against aggro lists. Lockdown buys time, Ride's End handles threats, Stock Up brings answers, and Regent can win the game.
Doesn't have enough lands to progress the game plan and would have to rely solely on card draw to find a third land.

early_game: Play defensively and use Temporary Lockdown to buy time against early threats
Prioritize finding lands and early interaction like Ride's End
Turn 1: Play a land, cast a cantrip or cheap interaction if available.
Turn 2: Develop mana, cast a one-mana cantrip/filter, or deploy a one-mana removal/counter.
If drawing Three Steps Ahead or Consult the Star Charts early, use them to dig for answers or set up the bottom of your library.
Land sequencing: Prioritize fetching/play lands that enter untapped to ensure consistent second lands and avoid mana screw.
mid_game: Stabilize the board using removal and counters
Generate card advantage with Stock Up and Three Steps Ahead to find answers
Develop token engines like Overlord of the Mistmoors and Beza, the Bounding Spring
Treat mana as a resource for answers, not just threats.
Hold open mana for interaction until the opponent commits.
Use Three Steps Ahead and Consult the Star Charts to filter dead draws and find answers to specific threats.
control wins by trading resources efficiently.
Protect your own graveyard from hate, but be aware of opponent recursion or graveyard synergies.
late_game: Reach inevitability by controlling everything that happens
Use Elspeth, Storm Slayer's abilities to create tokens and remove threats
Cast Jace, the Perfected Mind's ultimate to win by deck out

The game is won by out-resourcing the opponent through card advantage and efficient removal, culminating in a decisive board wipe followed by a resilient threat or a series of filtered draws to find the winning answer.
Day of Judgment combined with a late-game threat or card advantage engine often closes the game.
Go all-in when you have established card advantage, the opponent has no interaction in hand, or when you've forced them to use their key answers.
Commit to a threat when you can protect it or when the board state is favorable.
Don't overcommit if the opponent still has unanswered interaction.

key_cards.name: Elspeth, Storm Slayer
key_cards.notes: Creates creature tokens for protection, interacts with threats, and removes enemy creatures with its third ability.

key_cards.name: Jace, the Perfected Mind
key_cards.notes: Wins games outside of combat and can use its ultimate to make the opponent lose by decking out.

key_cards.name: Stock Up
key_cards.notes: Looks at the top 5 cards and puts two in hand, nearly always providing a useful tool.

key_cards.name: Temporary Lockdown
key_cards.notes: Main weapon against aggro decks in the first few turns, buying time to deal with threats.

key_cards.name: Ride's End
key_cards.notes: Handles threats that come after early game defenses.

key_cards.name: Overlord of the Mistmoors
key_cards.notes: Synergizes exceptionally with Elspeth by putting more tokens into play.

key_cards.name: Beza, the Bounding Spring
key_cards.notes: Versatile token producer that performs well, particularly against aggro decks.

sequencing_tips: Don't rush things and let the opponent think they control the game before taking control back.
Save resources and try to lead the opponent into making a mistake.
Never pass a turn without enough mana to interact.
Don't let the opponent force you to play something at the end of your turn.
Common mistake: Overusing Day of Judgment too early or too late.
Use it to maximize value, not just as a panic button.
Another mistake: Hoarding mana and missing interaction windows.
Unique interaction: Three Steps Ahead and Consult the Star Charts can be used to set up the bottom of your library with answers to specific threats, effectively turning your library into a sideboard.
Be mindful of format-specific hate cards that can disrupt your game plan.

matchups.advice: Use Temporary Lockdown and Ultima carefully. Don't rush things and let the opponent think they control the game before taking control back.
Play similarly to Mono-Red but be careful since Izzet uses blue and can play slightly differently to control you instead.
Focus on disrupting the interaction between Abuelo's Awakening and Omniscience. Watch out for their alternative game plan.
Save resources, lead the opponent into mistakes, never pass without enough mana to interact, and avoid being forced to play at end of turn.
Deal with Zur, Eternal Schemer whenever possible. Use Get Lost to handle threats and be aware of ETB effects like Overlords.

Use multiple cheap threats or layered spells to force counters. Utilize cantrips to dig past counters. If possible, use artifact/enchantment spells or instant-speed threats that are harder to counter. Save your own counters for key spells and avoid overcommitting to the stack.

Prioritize early interaction to disrupt mana development. Use Day of Judgment to reset when they commit to a big threat. Bring in artifact destruction and hand disruption. Don't trade resources; focus on stopping their ramp engines and out-value them with card selection.

Stabilize with cheap removal and counters. Use Day of Judgment if they establish a wide board. Filter aggressively to find answers. Don't overcommit to the board; control the pace until you can close. Prioritize life total early and don't let them stabilize with cheap blockers.

Apply pressure with resilient threats. Use card advantage to out-value them. Force them to use answers on less important spells. Use Three Steps Ahead and Consult the Star Charts to dig for the winning combination. Don't let them out-grind you; play efficiently and punish missteps.

Win by out-drawing and out-resolving. Prioritize card advantage spells. Save counters for their key threats or win conditions. Use Day of Judgment strategically to reset when you're ahead on board or when they're low on answers. Track their hand size and graveyard to predict their game plan.

common_threats: Watch for mana development (ramp vs control), hand size (combo vs control), and graveyard activity (recursion vs control).

overview_evidence.evidence_span: Azorius Control wins by manipulating the match well enough to tip the scales in its favor. It will eventually control everything that happens in the game and reach inevitability.

win_conditions_evidence.evidence_span: Elspeth, Storm Slayer is this deck's main win condition.
Another interesting win condition this list plays that is often an alternative to Elspeth is Jace, the Perfected Mind.
Regent can both draw you another card and win the game for you.

sequencing_tips_evidence.evidence_span: Don't rush things and let your opponent think they control the game. That's when you'll get control back again with Azorius.
Save your resources and try to lead your opponent into making a mistake. Never pass a turn without enough mana to interact, and don't let them force you to play something at the end of your turn.

common_threats_evidence.evidence_span: Screaming Nemesis and Monstrous Rage. Nemesis is the core of red aggro strategies... Monstrous Rage, on the other side, can simply end games
Magebane Lizard is very annoying, perhaps just as much as the main drops in Red.
With Drake Hatcher and Cori-Steel Cutter, Izzet should dominate the format
Zur, Eternal Schemer is also critical for them, so try to deal with it whenever possible.
